policy_statement: Energy adaptation strategy: promote energy plantations to reduce pressure on natural forests, regulate sustainable biomass energy use, support community-based off-grid/mini-grids, conserve/protect water catchments for hydropower and municipal supply, and prevent sedimentation affecting hydropower generation.
mechanism_description: Conservation and protection of water catchments and efforts to prevent sedimentation help maintain hydroelectric generation and support reliable municipal water supply.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (drought/intense rainfall/erosion) → catchment degradation & sedimentation risk → service disruption risk (hydropower/water supply) → catchment protection → ↓ exposure/sensitivity → ↑ service resilience
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system: Land and watershed management is altered via conservation/protection of catchments (e.g., restricting degrading land uses, restoring/maintaining vegetative cover, controlling erosion in upstream areas feeding reservoirs/intakes).  
(2) Mediating process: Improved infiltration and flow regulation, reduced soil erosion and sediment transport, and improved raw water quality reduce reservoir siltation and protect turbines/intakes; this stabilizes dry-season flows and moderates flood-driven turbidity spikes.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Increased reliability/resilience of hydroelectric generation and municipal water supply under climate variability and extremes (droughts, intense rainfall, landslides/erosion), reducing outage risk and water shortages.
dominant_pathway_test: Protecting water catchments most strongly reduces climate-related disruption risk to hydropower generation and municipal water supply by stabilizing runoff and reducing sedimentation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism targets climate and hydrology-driven risks (variable flows, droughts, heavy-rain erosion) that threaten hydropower and water-supply service continuity, and uses catchment protection to reduce sensitivity and exposure.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While catchment conservation can also sequester carbon (Nature-Based Solutions) and can indirectly avoid fossil backup generation (Mitigation), the stated and generally largest intended climate-relevant effect for this mechanism is maintaining reliable energy/water services under climate stress—an adaptation outcome.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy’s principal quantified objective were carbon sequestration or renewable expansion via increased generation (displacing fossil fuels), that would shift dominant effect; however, those would be different mechanisms or require policy-level evidence. Here, “resilience/reliability” and “prevent sedimentation affecting generation” are explicitly adaptation-focused.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy framed as catchment protection to maintain hydropower/water supply reliability functions through the same resilience pathway (reduced sedimentation/regulated flows), so Adaptation fits across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any secondary tagging (e.g., NBS co-benefits, mitigation co-benefits, biodiversity) depends on the specific policy design and evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Catchment protection often overlaps with Nature-Based Solutions (carbon sinks, biodiversity) and can yield mitigation via avoided fossil backup generation; classify as Adaptation unless the dominant stated/quantified outcome is emissions reduction/carbon sequestration rather than resilience of hydropower/water supply.
key_indicators: “Adaptation strategies of the energy sector”; “Support the promotion and implementation of energy plantation schemes”; “Develop a system to regulate the sustainable use of biomass energy”; “Promote and support the development… of… off-grid/mini-grids”; “Conservation and protection of water catchments… around hydro-power and municipal water supply sources”; “Prevent sedimentation… in hydroelectric facilities”
co_benefits: Reduced deforestation/forest degradation; biodiversity and ecosystem services protection; improved air quality/health from cleaner energy access; rural electrification and energy security; improved municipal water quality
instance_edge_case_notes: This entry bundles multiple instrument logics (regulation of biomass use; support for mini-grids; catchment protection measures). With no explicit legal mandate, quantified targets, or stated budget, it is treated as a planning/strategy instrument rather than a regulatory-mandate or infrastructure-investment.
